title: Holomorphic families of Riemann surfaces and asymptotic group theory
speaker: Thomas Delzant
field_msc: 32 Several complex variables and analytic spaces
tags: Workshop on Fundamental Groups and Periods

proof_stages.label: Problem and group-theoretic target
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines non-isotrivial holomorphic families of Riemann surfaces over a fixed compact Kähler manifold, recalls classical finiteness results, and announces the goal of showing that the number of such families depends only on the fundamental group of the base.

proof_stages.label: Monodromy, classifying maps, and curve factorization
proof_stages.summary: A family is encoded by its monodromy representation into the mapping class group and by an equivariant Teichmüller-space classifying map, and the key special case is when the family is pulled back from a family over a hyperbolic two-orbifold.

proof_stages.label: Cohomological detection of factorization
proof_stages.summary: Using the Weil–Petersson class on Teichmüller space and the contractibility of Teichmüller space, the speaker explains that factorization through a curve is equivalent to the classifying map having complex rank one, equivalently to vanishing of the square of the pulled-back Weil–Petersson class, hence is determined by monodromy.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to a monodromy finiteness dichotomy
proof_stages.summary: For a fixed group γ, the central proposition is formulated: any infinite sequence of family monodromies γ→Mod(S) has all but finitely many terms factoring through curves, so the non-curve-factorizing monodromies form a finite set.

proof_stages.label: Kähler-group fibrations from quotients and tree actions
proof_stages.summary: The talk recalls that suitable quotients of a Kähler group onto hyperbolic two-orbifold groups are holomorphically realized as fibrations, and that the Gromov–Schoen theorem turns nontrivial actions of Kähler groups on real trees into such orbifold fibrations.

proof_stages.label: Asymptotic real-tree construction
proof_stages.summary: After reviewing ways to obtain tree actions, the speaker defines real trees and δ-hyperbolicity in first-order metric terms and constructs an ultralimit tree by rescaling a divergent sequence of group actions on a hyperbolic space by its energy.

proof_stages.label: Mapping-class-group hyperbolic actions and conclusion
proof_stages.summary: The final step is to apply the asymptotic-tree mechanism to suitable hyperbolic spaces on which the mapping class group acts, refining the curve-complex action with recent spaces that force the original monodromies themselves to factor through the orbifold fibration, yielding the announced finiteness and dependence only on γ.

examples.name: Curve-base topological dependence theorem
examples.note: The earlier result for families over a Riemann-surface base illustrates the desired phenomenon that counts of non-isotrivial families can depend only on topology, motivating the stronger fundamental-group statement.
examples.engagement_reason: He states the curve-base theorem as a motivating known result that the number of nonisotrivial families depends only on the base topology, without proving or applying it concretely.

examples.name: Corlette–Simpson PSL2(C) representation dichotomy
examples.note: The speaker compares the mapping-class-group problem with Corlette–Simpson’s theorem for Zariski-dense Kähler-group representations into PSL2(C), where valuations produce tree actions; it is a parallel illustration of the same tree strategy rather than a proof step here.
examples.engagement_reason: He sketches how the Corlette–Simpson PSL2(C) dichotomy is proved, passing to coefficient fields, valuations, tree actions, and then recovering factorization through a curve.

passes.reason: the Corlette–Simpson PSL2(C) dichotomy is cited as an analogous classification result, with no counterexample role or intrinsic special-object structure flagged

examples.name: The usual hyperbolic plane
examples.note: The hyperbolic disk/plane is used to show that a Gromov-hyperbolic space may have thin triangles without being a tree, clarifying why the limiting parameter δ→0 is needed.
examples.engagement_reason: He uses the usual hyperbolic plane as a concrete case of a delta-hyperbolic space, reasoning with large triangles and the inscribed-circle radius/log-2 bound.

passes.reason: the hyperbolic plane is a maximally symmetric constant-curvature model used to show that a Gromov-hyperbolic space need not be a tree
